data.title: nCino: Strong Bookings And Healthy FCF Ahead (Upgrade)
data.description:
So far in 2026, few segments of the stock market have been as hard hit as the small- and mid-cap software space. Investors fearing the "SaaSpocalypse" have been dumping these stocks to multi-year lows, concerned that the prevalence of generalist AI tools will begin to unseat incumbent recurring-revenue business models.

And yet, actual fundamentals coming from these companies' most recent quarterly reports suggest that any AI infraction is still a long way off, if it's coming at all. nCino (NCNO), a vertical software company that specializes in the financial services industry, recently shot up more than 10% post-earnings after the company reported strong bookings and is forecasting top-line acceleration in the year ahead. Even after this jump, the stock remains down ~30% since the start of the year and ~40% over the trailing 12 months, and in my view, the post-earnings rally could be the beginning of a deeper upward re-rating.

I last wrote a "Neutral" article on nCino in December, when the stock was still trading at $25 per share. Since then, not only has nCino's valuation fallen, but the company has delivered tremendous results as well. Strong bookings and ACV are prompting excellent guidance for the upcoming year FY27, while nCino is also delivering terrific cash flow. I'm upgrading nCino to a "Buy" rating here.

For investors who are newer to nCino, here is what I consider to be the core drivers of the bull case for the company:

  • nCino serves a highly regulated industry, which is unlikely to uproot its software systems in favor of AI. nCino's top customers are some of the largest banks in the U.S. and abroad. The tangle of regulation surrounding banks makes it very unlikely that they will be willing to experiment with AI to displace existing systems that work.
  • nCino addresses a large $19.5 billion SAM and has substantial overseas growth potential. The company estimates its serviceable addressable market (SAM) at $19.5 billion. Meanwhile, its current annual revenue run rate (guidance calling for ~$640 million in revenue in FY27) indicates that the company's penetration into this market is just over 3%. The company has also made deeper inroads in landing significant Spanish and Japanese clients.
  • Top-tier customers. The blue-chip banks that nCino serves include Wells Fargo & Company (WFC), Barclays, Bank of America Corporation (BAC), and U.S. Bancorp (USB); and overseas, the company has landed Santander (SAN), one of the largest banks in Spain and Latin America.
  • Rising economies of scale. The company's pro forma operating margins have leaped to the low/mid-20%s, which stacks well on top of near-10% growth to position nCino just under the cusp of the Rule of 40.

And despite the post-earnings rally, nCino's share price erosion since the start of the year still leaves the stock at very attractive multiples, especially against a healthy guidance for FY27. At current share prices near $17, the stock trades at a market cap of $1.72 billion. Netting off the $130.2 million of cash and $218.9 million of debt on nCino's most recent balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $1.81 billion.

For FY27, as shown in the chart above, nCino has guided to $639-$643 million (7%-8% y/y) in revenue. Notably, the company is also expecting $132-$137 million in free cash flow (21% margin), or 63% y/y FCF growth. This positions nCino's valuation at:

  • 2.8x EV/FY27 FCF
  • 13.4x EV/FY27 FCF

In my view, the ability to buy a recurring-revenue software company in an attractive industry vertical that is fairly resistant to AI at just a low-teens FCF multiple is quite appealing. Stay long here and use the post-earnings rally as a buying opportunity.

Q4 download

Let's now go through nCino's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

nCino's revenue grew 6% y/y to $149.7 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $147.6 million (+4% y/y) by a two-point margin. Subscription revenue of $133.4 million grew at a stronger 7% y/y pace. Note here that the company faced a one-time headwind from a contract buyout in the fourth quarter of last year; excluding this headwind, the company notes that subscription revenue growth would have been even stronger at 10% y/y.

Importantly, nCino closed out FY26 with 17% y/y growth in ACV (annual contract value), reflecting a surge of new bookings that will translate into healthy revenue growth in FY27.

Net revenue retention rates also clocked in at 110%, indicating that the average customer is expanding their spending on the nCino platform by 10%. Management notes that new business activity has been especially strong in overseas markets like Japan, while large existing customers continue to expand their contract values - again, none of these bullish remarks indicates that nCino is at risk of being displaced by AI anytime soon.

Per CEO Sean Desmond's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

First, in the U.S. enterprise market, we delivered our best sales quarter in over 4 years, which included a mortgage expansion with a top 40 bank and cross-selling commercial to our largest consumer lending customer [...] I'm also thrilled with the momentum we continue to see in Japan, as highlighted by the fourth quarter signing of one of the largest banks in the world for a commercial lending transformation. I want to congratulate the Japanese team for tripling their total ACV in fiscal '26 from fiscal '25. Third, it's gratifying to see our existing customers continue to validate our AI strategy as they move to our new platform pricing framework to access our growing AI capabilities.

We saw expanded commitments from some of our largest accounts, and our ACV net retention rate improved to 112% or 109% organically and in constant currency, up from 106% in fiscal '25. Consistent with what we saw throughout fiscal '26, we closed a number of early renewals in the fourth quarter, including a fresh 5-year commitment from our largest customer by ACV. And those customer commitments go beyond dollars. Critically, they come with trust.

The company additionally noted that churn rates fell to a 3-year low. We note that the company's revenue guidance for FY27 has a 4% buffer for churn.

On the profitability front, nCino's pro forma operating margins rose 6 points y/y to 23%, reflecting economies of scale on a larger subscription revenue base and a favorable revenue mix shift away from unprofitable professional services.

The company's Q4 FCF also shot up to an 8% margin, with substantial FCF growth expected in the year ahead.

Key takeaways

nCino's fourth-quarter update, and its robust guidance outlook for FY27, help to dispel the notion that the company is at any near-term risk of being ousted by AI. We note that the ability to buy this stock at just ~13x FCF is quite compelling. Keep riding this stock's post-earnings momentum higher.

data.title: SPE Vs. BRW: High Yields, Weak Compounding -- Neither Is Cheap Enough
data.description:
In this article, I will discuss two funds that both work by buying CEFs for cheap, harvesting the income, and also making real gains when the discounts narrow. These funds - Special Opportunities Fund, Inc. (SPE) and Saba Capital Income & Opportunities Fund (BRW) - both look attractive, with double digit yields and double digit discounts.

Comparing these two CEFs is particularly relevant today because the CEF space is once again seeing wide discounts after recent rate volatility and credit repricing. This is more so because both SPE and BRW are explicitly designed to exploit such changes. So the discounts do exist, but it is important to understand which structure is better suited to exploit them and convert them into realized returns in the current macro scenario.

The key difference is in how they get there. Being a “fund of funds,” SPE invests 68.3% of assets in other closed-end funds. It invests another 18.2% in SPACs. It pays a managed distribution fixed at 8% of prior year-end NAV. So this is an engineered payout, and investors are exposed to fee layering - meaning the fund has its own fees on top of the fees it has to pay to the funds it owns.

SPE’s expense ratio is reported as ~1.86%. However, it is a fund-of-funds, meaning it also pays expense fees to the CEFs it owns. Even though they are not part of the reported expense ratio, investors are, obviously, exposed to them. Assuming 70% CEF exposure and an average expense ratio of 0.50%, that adds an indirect 0.35% to the expense ratio.

BRW is a more direct – and aggressive – vehicle. It holds CEFs, credit, private credit, and other opportunistic assets. It also often has an activist overlay aimed to force value out of its holdings. That makes BRW more opportunistic and complex, but it also comes with a bumpier ride and much greater complexity.

BRW reports a much higher expense ratio of ~5.8%. This is not a fee layering like SPE, but it comes from direct costs tied to leverage, derivatives and complex strategies. Even without layering, this expense ratio is so high that it creates a tough return hurdle all by itself for investors.

At current discounts, neither fund is cheap given their individual drawbacks.

Where the Yield Actually Comes From

So both SPE and BRW monetize discount narrowing - that is their common feature. But they have critical differences. When you dig into the actual holdings of the two funds, that difference becomes much clearer.

SPE is a fund whose income comes from other funds. Its top holdings are CEFs like SRH Total Return Fund (~11.1%), General American Investors (~10.6%), and Central Securities (~6.2%). Many of the CEFs it owns themselves use leverage and managed distributions. The SPACs are event-driven positions, so their earnings are lumpy.

SPE’s income is primarily indirect, because much of its allocations is in other funds. To that, they are adding capital gains from buying CEFs at a discount to NAV (say $0.90 for $1.00 of assets). If that discount narrows (say to $0.95 or $1.00), the fund makes a capital gain, even if the underlying assets don’t move much. They are supplementing both distributions and capital gains from what they earn through SPAC arbitrage and event-driven trades. Their MDP allows payouts to be funded by income, realized gains, or even capital if necessary.

To explain this in simple terms — SPE is a second order CEF. It does not primarily generate income from underlying assets. Its income mainly comes from other managers’ distributions and from discount narrowing.

BRW owns income-producing assets and generates more direct income. Here’s the breakdown:

Most of these are not passive holdings, these are targeted positions tied to activist campaigns or discount opportunities.

Coupon and spread income, carry trades and derivatives form a large part of BRW’s income. So these are actual, direct incomes — unlike SPE’s — but there are trade-offs. Some of these assets are less liquid, quite complex, and change with credit cycles. On top of that are derivatives, which add another layer of complexity.

In direct contrast to SPE, BRW is a first-order generator of income. Income comes directly from credit exposure, structured products, and active trades, rather than from distribution from other funds.

Distribution Stability — What You Actually Get Paid

SPE pays 8% of the prior year-end NAV as baseline payout. That comes to ~$0.1087 per month (~$1.30 annually). In 2025, this went up to $2.70 — a 2x+ jump YoY. That extra amount came from special distributions. Some months, like in the latter months of 2025, the extra distribution came from realized capital gains. Some months, like in the earlier parts of 2025, nearly 90% came from ROC.

I should note that the payout itself remained unchanged — only its classification changed. Even then, it makes the income appear uneven and difficult to interpret.

This difference is due to the timing of the realized gains; however, it still makes the income event-driven and uneven.

At BRW, the picture is different. BRW has a stable payout by design, but an unstable strategy. Here, returns come from uneven opportunities. That is smoothed out at the fund level, but the underlying performance remains lumpy. Current distribution is about $1.02 annually (~$0.085 monthly), implying a ~15.4% forward yield.

However, true distribution does not appear to grow, despite them reporting a 5-year dividend growth rate of ~24%. Despite that, the fund shows 0 years of consistent dividend growth. That means payouts must have been adjusted, not steadily increased. Payouts jump a lot across years. So for example, there was a 96% jump between 2021 and 2022, and a 24% cut in 2024. So the payout does not compound, but it driven by policy that adjusts based on realized income.

So, to clarify the difference, both SPE and BRW have uneven sources of income. SPE shows you the volatility. BRW hides it.

BRW’s market performance cannot hide the underlying volatility. Over the past year, its price return has been negative at -14.6%. Total return has been barely positive at times.

Performance Reality — Neither Is A Compounder

The best way to compare these two funds is by separating their price return from their total return. Now, in the past one year, SPE lost 10.8% on price but still managed a positive 6.95 total return when distributions are added. BRW, on the other hand, lost 14.71% on price and produced a negative 2% total return.

This is the sharpest contrast between SPE and BRW. SPE’s distributions were able to offset its capital loss. Even though it was small, investors still did have a net positive total return. BRW’s distributions were unable to offset the price destruction.

BRW pays 15.43% while SPE pays 9.6% (current forward yield), so at first glance, BRW’s total returns should have outperformed SPE. But it didn’t, and the difference is due to realized gains. SPE must have done better - we saw that in the huge increase in distribution last year.

Equally unflattering is the medium term picture. Over three years, SPE’s price return was 25.6% while total return was a much higher 79.7% - a large difference explained by the large payout. BRW, on the other hand, had a negative 16.8% price return while total return was a positive 26.8% when accounting for distributions.

Thus, neither fund acts like a true compounder. SPE makes gains and passes them on, but leaves price performance weak. BRW makes smooth payouts, but price performs worse because it remains under constant pressure.

So the takeaway is easy: SPE has been the better holding, but neither are good holdings per se if you are looking for price appreciation. SPE has performed relatively better recently, but that is only because it did a better job recently converting opportunistic gains into distributable cash.

This is the core issue going forward: yield alone is not sufficient. The fund needs to continuously generate realizable gains if it wants to sustain that yield while not eroding NAV at the same time.

Forward-Looking View — What Happens Next

In the next 12–24 months, the three key drivers of returns will be: rates, credit conditions, and CEF discount behavior. Both SPE and BRW are exposed to all three, but in very different ways.

If rates stabilize or fall, liquidity improves and discounts narrow. In that case, SPE benefits directly, as even a 3–5% narrowing in CEF discounts translates to significant realizable gains for the fund. BRW here benefits indirectly, only through tighter credit spreads and better asset valuations.

If rates stay higher for longer, discounts will remain wide, so SPE may buy well, but won’t be able to monetize positions. As for BRW, increased credit stress will directly impact its portfolio.

The metric where they diverge is credit risk. BRW is directly exposed to it through private credit, structured products and spread compression. SPE’s exposure is indirect. Its holdings face direct exposure, while its own returns will be driven by how those holdings are rerated – not directly by credit risk.

In a downturn, this impacts them differently. BRW faces NAV pressure directly, plus it has to make higher payouts. SPE only faces slower realizations, so it cannot make the excess distributions. However, its baseline payout, tied to NAV, is lower now. Put simply, BRW is forced to keep paying, while SPE is forced to wait.

The real test in a downturn is yield sustainability. BRW’s ~15% yield requires continuous income and gains. If those fall short, NAV erosion happens – or cuts. SPE’s yield is lower, and since they are structurally tied to NAV, they are easier to sustain. Of course, if realizations slow down significantly, SPE’s excess distribution does disappear, and NAV can also erode slowly.

So what we understand here is that SPE needs volatility and discount movement, while BRW needs stable credit and strong execution. In today’s uncertain, late-cycle environment, SPE’s model is better aligned.

Why The Discount Continues to Exist

Despite the high yields, both funds continue to trade at significant discounts. There are reasons for that.

The first problem is distribution quality. Neither SPE nor BRW’s distribution is consistent, or derived from consistent income. BRW does look smoother, but that’s only because its choppy returns are smoothened out by policy. Such inconsistency does not help these securities command premium valuations.

The second problem is that both funds are a little too complex. SPE operates as a fund-of-funds, which means there are two layers of fees - SPE’s own fees, and fees of its holding funds. Investors also have indirect exposure to leverage and second-layer distribution policies that reduce transparency. BRW’s complexity is different. Its exposure across private credit, structured products, derivatives and so on are harder to value and less liquid. They are also more sensitive to credit cycles. This complexity tends to be priced at a discount.

Lack of proper compounding is the third issue. Neither fund delivers decent price appreciation in the long run. They mostly pay out all their returns. In SPE’s case, much of the earlier months may be 80-90% ROC, and then the latter months may be 0 ROC. That was the case in 2025. In 2026, so far, there has been no ROC. This makes the source of distribution confusing, and the share price is punished as a result.

A fourth issue is cost. BRW’s ~5.8% expense ratio is not insignificant. In fact it is a major structural drag, consuming a significant portion of gross returns before customers see even a dollar.

Finally, a reason for the discount could be lack of trust in execution. Both strategies depend on management’s continued ability to realize gains. When the execution becomes uneven — as we saw with BRW recently — the market may discount the stock as a measure of safety.

In effect, both funds come discounted because investors do not fully trust either to consistently deliver returns.

Conclusion

Both SPE and BRW buy assets at discounts, make an income, and realize opportunistic gains. How they actually do that makes the difference.

SPE is more controlled, with uneven returns, but gains all converted to cash and distributed. BRW is more aggressive, with higher yield through a variety of risky bets, but with higher volatility and weak performance recently.

Between the two, SPE is the better holding today, since it has been able to convert opportunistic gains into distributable cash better than BRW. It also carries less direct exposure to credit risk in an uncertain macro environment.

BRW’s higher yield looks attractive, but it comes at higher execution risk and greater sensitivity to macro problems. If we don’t see a clear improvement in realized returns, the yield alone doesn’t look sufficient.

The key idea is simple though: neither is a strong compounder, neither is cheap enough to justify the risks. Between the two, SPE is the better-structured fund in the current environment, despite its own limitations.

data.title: Snap Shares Surge On Activist Letter: Why This Could Be A Stock Bargain Now
data.description:
Back in August 2025, I wrote a bullish article suggesting that Snap Inc. (SNAP) shares were worth buying after dropping from a high of around $80 to just $7. My bullishness has not paid off, and the stock recently dropped to below $4. The stock seemed to be in freefall for the past month as the stock market went into a correction due to the war in Iran. Plus, there was an unfavorable legal case for Meta Platforms (META) which negatively impacted social media stocks. Basically, it seems like Snap shares have been in a perfect storm.

If I feel I was wrong about a stock I am willing to cut my losses and sell. But, if I think the market is wrong, I will often buy more shares when a stock declines, and that is exactly what I did when Snap shares plunged into the $3 range. I believe that because of a lot of negative sentiment, Snap shares have become disconnected from the value it has in the mid- to long-term time frame scenario. I think there is a lot of value in Snap and that management needs to change course.

Just a couple of weeks ago, I wrote an article about how AI-related job losses could hurt the economy but that some companies like Snap could be poised to announced major layoffs and potentially massively increase earnings in the coming years. My article pointed out that Meta Platforms and Block (XYZ) had just announced big layoffs and that I believed Snap would soon do the same. It seems that other investors are seeing this potential as well, because activist shareholder Irenic just sent an open letter to Snap's management team with this and other suggestions that could potentially create significant shareholder upside.

With all of this in mind, let's take a look at why I keep buying Snap shares, and how some potential changes at the company could drive this stock much higher:

The Chart

As shown in the chart below, Snap shares were trading for about $9 per share just a few weeks ago and it is really hard to see anything that has happened with this company in that time frame that could make sense of such a massive decline. While it has not been a great environment for most stocks lately and Meta Platforms did lose a legal case that raises some concerns about social media stocks, I believe a big part of the decline has just been based on fear and panic based selling, more so than on the fundamentals of this company.

Even after a rebound from the recent lows due to the letter from an activist investor, this stock is still trading way below key support levels. This includes the 50-day moving average which is $5.39 and the 200-day moving average which is around $7.43. If the war in Iran wraps up soon, the selling pressure on stocks could ease and I think Snap shares could rebound back to at least the 50-day moving average if not the 200-day moving average fairly soon.

The Lawsuit That Impacted Meta Platforms And Other Social Media Stocks

There have a been a couple of legal cases that have created negative headlines. In March 2026, Meta Platforms and YouTube were found liable by a jury due to the addictive design of their platforms. A 20-year old woman was apparently unable to control her choices because these platforms have features like "infinite scroll," "autoplay," and reward notifications that she said led to her depression and body dysmorphia. The jury awarded her $3 million plus another $3 million in punitive damages, for a total of $6 million. This case and jury was based in California, and the defendants are expected to appeal this decision. Snap was a defendant in this case as well, but it settled before the case went to trial.

Investors seem concerned that this case will lead to more plaintiffs claiming that their depression and other problems in life were caused by social media. In addition to this legal setback, a jury in New Mexico ordered Meta Platforms to pay $375 million for violating state consumer protection laws by misleading parents about safety and for not preventing sexual exploitation of minors.

I believe these cases will likely be overturned during the appeals process, especially since social media companies have some significant legal defenses that could lead to a victory for the social media industry. One defense is "Section 230" of the Communications Decency Act which is meant to protect platforms from being sued for what content creators post. In this recent legal case, the plaintiffs argued that Meta Platforms has a "defective" product because of features like infinite scroll, which allowed this case to bypass section 230, but that might not hold true on appeal. Social media companies are also protected by the First Amendment for which Meta Platforms suggests that their algorithms are entitled to have protection, in the form of editorial discretion. Another defense is the idea that mental health in teens is very complex and influenced by hundreds of factors including family health history, school, upbringing, etc., and that depression and self-image problems cannot be pinned on a single app.

An Activist Investor Suggests Snap Shares Could Be Worth $26+

On March 31, 2026, a major shareholder sent an open letter to the CEO of Snap. The letter pointed out that Snap shares have significantly underperformed major market indexes since its IPO. It also pointed out that since Meta Platforms announced its "year of efficiency" in 2022, it has outperformed Snap by 477 percentage points. It also noted that Snap currently trades for about 1.2x revenue, while Meta Platforms trades for around 5.2x revenue. Many other valuation metrics were brought up, and the letter stated:

"More than that, in simplest terms, it seems to us that it is more than passing strange that Snap – with nearly 1 billion MAUs, reaching 75% of users aged 13-34 globally, with 350 million of those users engaging with AR tools, with users opening the app 40 times a day, with 25 million paying subscribers approaching $1 billion of ARR, with 5 billion+ snaps created daily, and with a massive library of image and video data, much of which is tied to geolocation – can be bought for just $7.2 billion (Snap’s enterprise value as of March 27th, 2026). To us, and I suspect to you too, this is a comically small sum."

Irenic Capital believes that a number of actions could be taken which could lead to Snap shares being valued at more than $26 per share and possibly more. This includes spinning off or shutting down "Specs," which is said to be burning around $500 million in cash annually. Irenic Capital also says that Snap needs to change the cost structure of the company through reducing the number of employees it has, and it cited the recent layoffs at Block as an example. It also said Snap needs to reduce stock based compensation, monetize AI, improve corporate governance and buy back shares if cash flow allows.

Earnings Estimates And The Balance Sheet

The earnings estimates below are provided by Seeking Alpha, and they show that Snap is expected to turn profitable in 2026 with earnings of $0.50 per share. This suggests a price to earnings ratio of just around 8, which is extremely cheap, especially for a tech stock. As shown below, earnings and revenues are expected to post solid growth in 2027 and 2028. I believe that if Snap is able to use AI to replace a significant number of employees like Block and Meta Platforms have announced, it could earn far more than what the estimates below suggest:

As for the balance sheet, this company has about $4.14 billion in debt and around $2.94 billion in cash. I view this as a strong balance sheet with lots of cash, and most importantly is the possibility that Snap could be profitable going forward for the first time. That could lead to a situation where the company could pay down debt and even buy back shares as Irenic Capital is suggesting.

Other Points To Consider

Here are a few other points that should be considered when it comes to Snap:

  1. I am pleased to see Irenic Capital buying shares of Snap, and also seeing ideas about cost cutting which seem to align with my own views. I also viewed major investments in this company by Prince Alwaleed bin Talal as a sign of major potential upside, as he is said to own or control over 40 million shares which is nearly 3% of the company. However, I am troubled that I have not seen any insider buying in recent weeks, and multiple insiders have recently sold Snap shares even in the $4 range.
  2. The European Union announced an investigation into Snap to see if it was complying with the Digital Services Act. The investigation is said to be focused on Snap's age verification system and other child protection concerns. Based on the fact that Snap requires its users to be at least 13 years old and that it has additional privacy protections for anyone between age 13-17, I feel that the company has taken appropriate measures to protect teens under the Digital Services Act.
  3. Snap has a dual-class structure that gives founders like CEO Evan Spiegel total control. This limits the power of outside investors including shareholder activists like Irenic Capital.

Where I Could Be Wrong

I am concerned that Snap management will continue on the same path which has led to losses for many shareholders. If the company continues funding and losing money on potential "moonshots" like Specs, it could diminish profitability for years to come. This company is much smaller than Meta Platforms and YouTube and it does not have the same level of financial resources to fund R&D or to defend lawsuits if these addiction type legal claims keep coming and if they are not overturned on appeal.

Snap typically appeals to a younger audience which is ideal for some advertisers, but younger consumers also don't typically have as much spending power. This could limit Snap's appeal for advertisers. The U.S. economy appears to be slowing down and the job market has been weakening in recent months. This could lead to a recession, and that could result in ad spending cutbacks for Snap. Also, user growth has recently been slower in North America and if that trend continues, it could become a bigger concern for shareholders and advertisers.

In Summary

I view Snap as a higher risk but potentially very high reward stock. At this point, there is just around $4 worth of remaining downside, if the stock goes to zero. By contrast, I believe that a return to $26 per share is realistic as outlined by Irenic Capital, but only if management takes some major steps to reduce headcount and cut back on other expenses like Specs.

Even before Irenic Capital sent out its open letter, I was already thinking (and I believe other investors were as well), that Snap might announce major AI-related layoffs when it announces Q1 2026 earnings on April 27th, 2026. Block shares soared after that company announced major layoffs, and we know that Meta Platforms shares have seen significant gains when it announced a "year of efficiency" in 2022. I think it might be time for Evan Spiegel to do the same with Snap, and I believe this could be a major upside catalyst for this very depressed stock.

data.title: SOLZ: Structurally Inefficient Given The Emergence Of Spot Solana ETFs
data.description:
It has been a little over one year since the first futures-based exchange-traded funds tied to the price of Solana came to market, with the Volatility Shares Solana ETF (SOLZ) beginning to trade on March 20th, 2025. Unlike spot ETFs [which at the time of the fund’s release had not yet received regulatory approval], the Volatility Shares ETF tracks the price of Solana through futures contracts rather than holding SOL-USD directly.

Similar to other cryptocurrency spot-based exchange-traded funds, the release of futures-based ETFs served as a precursor for the eventual approval of spot Solana ETFs near the end of 2025. With that said, given the emergence of spot ETFs, the use case for funds like SOLZ has become limited. I argue that, due to the fund’s higher expense ratio, lack of access to staking yield, and structural inefficiencies, investors should largely steer clear of SOLZ. While I are generally optimistic about the prospects of the underlying cryptocurrency, I assign a “Sell” rating to the Volatility Shares Solana ETF.

In the current bear market, I acknowledge that, despite long-term fundamentals remaining intact for the underlying token, near-term price action may continue to face headwinds. Given the current risk-off market environment, I maintain hold ratings on both spot Solana ETFs and the underlying asset.

Unique Value Proposition of Solana Futures-Based ETF has Eroded With Emergence of Spot ETFs

SOLZ does not directly hold Solana tokens. Instead, according to the issuer, “[the fund uses] financial derivatives [primarily futures contracts and potentially swap agreements] to gain exposure to Solana's price movements.”

According to the fund’s prospectus, “SOLZ seeks long-term capital appreciation by actively managing positions in Solana futures traded on CFTC-regulated exchanges.” As a result, investors' returns are tied to the performance of the futures contracts rather than the underlying Solana itself.

Given the greater efficiency of spot ETFs, it is my opinion that the qualities of SOLZ have become structurally disadvantaged in the current environment. Volatility Shares acknowledges that the fund aims to provide investors 1X exposure to the price movements of Solana while avoiding many of the difficulties/complexities of direct crypto ownership. However, the question becomes, in the current market, why would an investor choose a futures-based fund rather than a spot-based fund?

SOLZ’s structural problems stem from its reliance on derivatives rather than holding spot Solana, which has resulted in deviations in performance over time. In particular, losses can occur from selling lower-priced expiring contracts and buying higher-priced, longer-dated ones. Over time, this can create a persistent drag on returns.

Additionally, a portion of the portfolio must remain in cash and treasuries as collateral, which limits the full participation in Solana’s upside price appreciation. When combined with higher fees and price tracking inefficiencies, these structural factors can lead to a return profile that fails to match that of the spot-based ETFs. These inefficiencies are structural rather than cyclical and therefore unlikely to improve over time.

The chart below shows that during periods of higher market volatility, SOLZ’s performance has deviated from that of both GSOL and SOL-USD. For example, performance between SOLZ and GSOL differed by approximately 34 percentage points in September 2025.

Another structural disadvantage comes in the form of SOLZ’s dividend. With a current dividend yield of 3.30%, this is derived from the cash and treasuries held by the fund rather than the underlying economics of the Solana network itself. In contrast, spot Solana ETFs that offer staking capabilities can generate yield directly from on-chain activity. Notably, given current staking yields (~6.2%), the potential income derived from staking is higher than the dividend provided by SOLZ. This makes SOLZ’s income stream both less competitive and structurally disconnected from the underlying asset.

While staking fees vary significantly across funds (see table below), current net yields for spot ETFs are still significantly greater than SOLZ’s current dividend yield. As an example, the Franklin Solana ETF (SOEZ) currently offers a net yield of 5.42%.

ETF

Staking Fees

BSOL

FSOL

15% of Solana rewards

VSOL

0.28% of total staked Solana

GSOL

23% of Solana rewards

(Source: etfdb)

One major drawback to the Volatility Shares Solana ETF is the expense ratio of 0.95%. When compared to spot ETFs, only the REX-Osprey SOL + Staking ETF (SSK) has a worse fee structure. As shown in the chart below, the Franklin Solana ETF (SOEZ) and the Bitwise Solana Staking ETF (BSOL) are currently the most affordable options for investors.

Additionally, as I have discussed in previous crypto ETF articles, AUM remains critically important given the burgeoning and highly competitive new asset class. By a wide margin, BSOL remains the spot Solana ETF leader, with just under $600 million in assets. That said, SOLZ maintains a respectable AUM of $101.20 million.

However, as crypto continues to navigate the current risk-off environment, I expect capital to likely migrate from futures-based ETFs like SOLZ to spot funds like BSOL, FSOL, and SOEZ as they prove to be more efficient vehicles to invest in the underlying crypto.

Given the introduction of spot Solana ETFs, the original purpose of SOLZ has largely been eliminated. Now, investors can invest directly from their brokerage accounts in funds that own the underlying asset. As a result, SOLZ now represents a structurally inferior and increasingly unnecessary option for investors seeking Solana exposure.

Risks to Underlying Thesis

Despite the structural drawbacks I have covered in this article, SOLZ may retain relevance for certain market participants.

For investors who are comfortable with exposure via derivatives, its accessibility via traditional brokerage accounts continues to provide a frictionless way to gain exposure to Solana’s price movements. SOLZ maintains respectable liquidity and AUM, supporting efficient trading and minimizing execution risk. Short-term traders or individuals seeking exposure without the complexities of spot ownership or staking may choose the futures-based fund over other available options.

Lastly, the fund’s collateral-driven yield derived from cash and treasuries could become relatively more attractive if staking yields were to decline and interest rates were to rise. This could potentially narrow the advantage currently held by spot-based staking ETFs. While I view SOLZ as structurally disadvantaged for long-term investors, it may still be appropriate in more tactical/constrained use cases.

Solana Network

The Solana network continues to be one of the leading L1s, with strong underlying on-chain metrics. Stablecoin supply remains near all-time highs of $15 billion, representative of a strong liquidity base. Likewise, TVL remains significantly elevated when compared to 2023 lows, currently sitting at approximately $6.4 billion.

Despite the current risk-off environment, Solana has retained a strong base of users and network activity, representative of its real economic value. With a demonstrated history of throughput advantages and clear use cases across DeFi, stablecoins, and RWAs, it is my opinion that it is likely to maintain its position among the leading cryptocurrencies. However, as I have seen in the past, price performance and network strength do not always move in tandem. In the short term, risk remains elevated.

While long-term fundamentals remain intact for the underlying token, near-term price action may continue to face headwinds. In the current market environment, the investment case for Solana remains mixed. While the network continues to show strong underlying activity, fundamentals such as active number of addresses and TVL have yet to translate into meaningful upward price movement amid the current risk-off backdrop. Given that crypto tends to underperform during periods of tightening financial conditions and reduced liquidity, I continue to remain cautious on the underlying crypto and spot ETFs.

Final Takeaway

While the Solana network continues to demonstrate strong fundamentals and long-term potential, SOLZ is no longer the best way for long-term investors to capture that upside. The fund’s reliance on futures introduces structural complications such as tracking inefficiencies and limited yield that is disconnected from the underlying network. When combined with a higher expense ratio, I view SOLZ’s return profile as being inherently less efficient relative to spot-based alternatives.

As the market continues to mature and capital flows toward more efficient structures, spot ETFs seem increasingly better suited for long-term investors. While I am generally optimistic about the long-term prospects of the underlying cryptocurrency, I assign a “Sell” rating to SOLZ.

data.title: Modine: Breakout Frenzy Meets AI Beneficiary Status - Downgrade To Hold
data.description:
I previously covered Modine Manufacturing Company (MOD) in January 2026, discussing why I had reiterated my Buy rating, thanks to the likely to be durable cooling demand across its diversified end markets and the top/bottom-line accretion from the expanded manufacturing capacity/improved pricing power.

This was despite the near-term risks from the potential sideways trading through FQ3'26, attributed to the pulled-forward upside potential and the elevated short interest ratio.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am downgrading the MOD stock to a Hold here, with the January 2026 breakout occurring overly fast/furiously while triggering the February 2026 bull trap and the still expensive stock valuations.

Pending a further correction to my Buy Zones, I am of the opinion that there remains a mixed risk/reward profile at current inflated levels, despite their profitable growth prospects during the multi-year data center capex boom along with the tailwinds from the upcoming Performance Technologies spin-off.

MOD Proves Its AI Beneficiary Status

Since my last Buy rating, MOD has had an outsized stock price performance while hitting a new 52-week high of $243s before returning some of those gains to retest the $200s by the time of writing.

If anything, readers may want to note that a similar breakout and moderation have also been observed in its direct thermal management peers in varying degrees.

1. Multi-Year Data Center Super Cycle

Much of their tailwinds may be attributed to the Q4'25 earnings season reiterating the durability of the AI spending trends, as the four leading hyperscalers "now expecting combined spending of close to $700 billion" on data center-related capex entering 2026.

These elevated spending trends are likely to be a multi-year cadence as well, as market analysts already project the global data center capacity to "nearly double from 103 GW to 200 GW by 2030," with it naturally triggering tailwinds for numerous data center-related infrastructure stocks, MOD included.

The same has been reported by MOD in the recent FQ3'26 earnings call, as the management raised their FY2026 net sales guidance to +22.5% YoY at the midpoint and adj. EBITDA to +18.5% YoY, against the original guidance of +6% YoY and +11% YoY offered in the FQ4'25 earnings call, respectively,

It is apparent that the management's intensified focus into the data center opportunities has paid off handsomely, as observed in the data center-related revenue growth by +78% YoY and the Climate Solutions' revenue growth by +50.9% YoY in FQ3'26.

This is significantly aided by the ongoing ramp-up of their capacity expansion, allowing them to capitalize on the ongoing cloud supercycle, as observed in the "31% sequential revenue growth for data center products in Q3, and we expect significant incremental volumes in the fourth quarter as well" albeit with a slight and temporary gross margin impact arising from "the capacity expansion for data center products."

2. Strategic Divestiture

As a result of Climate Solutions' richer gross profit margins of 24.8% in FQ3'26 (-3.8 points YoY/+5.5 from FY2019 levels of 19.3%, based on Commercial and Industrial Solutions and Building HVAC Systems), I can understand why MOD has decided to "spin off and combination of our Performance Technologies business with Gentherm."

This may allow the company to divest the slower-growth Performance Technologies (where vehicular end markets are embedded) at YTD revenue growth by -3.5% YoY compared to the Climate Solutions YTD growth by +28.7% YoY.

This is especially since Performance Technologies' YTD gross margins underwhelm at 18.6% (-1 point YoY/+4.8 from FY2019 levels of 13.8%) compared to Climate Solutions at 25.8% (-2.8 points YoY/+6.5 from FY2019 levels of 19.3%).

Pending the upcoming completion of the spinoff in Q4'26, I urge investors to temper their expectations from the near-term noise on MOD's performance metrics and the upcoming FY2027 guidance (to be offered in the upcoming FQ4'26 earnings call on May 20, 2026, post-market) from the slower growth Performance Technologies segment.

This is especially since MOD is likely to report an excellent pro forma performance metric upon the completed spin-off, as it "will become a pure-play climate solutions company, focused on the high-growth data center and commercial HVAC and refrigeration markets."

At the same time, readers may want to note that the spin-off will allow the management to use the $210M cash distribution to further reduce their debt leverage to a pro forma ratio of below 1x.

This is down from the current levels of 1.08x (compared to a year ago at 0.82x) based on the net debt levels of $517.1M in FQ3'26 (-80.1% YoY) and the annualized FQ3'26 adj. EBITDA of $478.4M (+36.9% YoY).

3. Profitable Growth Story Meets Expensive Valuations

Based on the:

  • YTD overall revenue performance of $2.22B (+15% YoY) and YTD adj. EPS performance of $3.31 (+12.9% YoY), aided by
  • the management's multi-year guidance of data center revenue growth at between +50% and +70%, and
  • the data center sales guidance of $2B in FY2028 (up from $1B guided for FY2026),

It is unsurprising that the consensus has already raised their forward estimates, with MOD expected to report accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +20.9%/+35.9% through FY2028.

This is compared to the historical growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +5.5%/+31% and a 10Y CAGR of +5.6%/+20.85%, respectively, with it apparent that the data center capex boom has been a boon for its growth prospects.

For now, despite the -17% correction from the 52-week highs and the raised consensus forward estimates, I am of the opinion that MOD still looks rather stretched at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 25.44x, given the ongoing upgrading from the 5Y mean of 11.38x and the sector median of 11.42x.

On the one hand, there is no denying its AI beneficiary status, as observed in the Climate Solutions' richer adj. EBITDA margins of 18.1% on a YTD basis (-2.8 points YoY), compared to the 11.4% observed in FY2021 and 11.7% in FY2020.

This is on top of the projected expansion to the 20% and 21% range in FQ4'26, with it signaling the company's highly profitable operations after the completion of the spin-off.

On the other hand, when comparing MOD's FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 25.44x and Climate Solutions' projected FQ4'26 adj. EBITDA guidance of 20.5% at the midpoint to its direct thermal management competitors, including:

  • AAON, Inc. (AAON) at 21.20x/16% in FY2025,
  • Johnson Controls (JCI) at 19.72x/17.1% over the LTM,
  • Trane Technologies (TT) at 19.85x/20.1% in FY2025,
  • Vertiv Holdings Co. (VRT) at 29.81x/20.3% in FY2025,
  • Carrier Global (CARR) at 12.18x/20.9% in FY2025, and
  • Lennox International Inc. (LII) at 13.73x/22.1% in FY2025, respectively,

It is apparent that MOD's recent rally by +212% from the 52-week lows and by +39.4% from my last Buy article has occurred overly fast and furiously, with the premium valuations offering interested investors a minimal margin of safety.

MOD Is Downgraded To A Hold Here

For now, MOD's double top bull trap has materialized by February 2026, with the two up/downtrend cycles observed in 2024/Q1'25 and Q2'25-December '25/January 2026 implying another up/downtrend cycle playing out at the time of writing.

This conjecture is not overly bearish as well, based on the notably reversing momentum after the prior overbought levels in mid-February 2026, with a further correction along the established base uptrend support line/200-day moving averages rather likely in the near term.

This is especially since the stock is still trading at a notable premium to my fair value estimates of $138.90, based on the annualized FQ3'26 adj. EPS of $4.76 (+29.3% YoY) and my last Buy rating FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 29.18x (not too far from the peer group current P/E mean of ~31x and the peer group 5Y P/E mean of ~26x).

Based on the consensus FY2028 adj. EPS estimates of $10.16, it is apparent that the stock has also pulled forward part of its upside potential to my long-term price target of $296.40, with a further correction necessary for an improved margin of safety.

Readers may want to note that MOD still boasts a relatively elevated short interest ratio of 7.15% as the stock market sentiments remain extremely pessimistic by the time of writing - with it implying potential volatility in the near term, pending further catalysts and/or the upcoming Q2'26 earnings season in April/May 2026.

Combined with the potential correction by -13.1% to my Buy Zones of between the March 2026 bottom of $186s and/or the Q4'25 top of $165s (still elevated compared to my fair value estimates of $138s), I prefer to cautiously downgrade the stock to a Hold here.

Some patience may be more prudent in the meantime.

data.title: Quanta Services: Leading The Way For U.S. Reindustrialization (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Quanta Services, Inc. (PWR) operates in one of the most robust market environments for engineering, procurement, and construction (EPC) work, underpinned by billions of dollars in large-scale data center development, U.S. reindustrialization, and the power infrastructure to support grid scalability. With the utilities expected to invest $1.1T through 2030 in power infrastructure, I believe Quanta is well positioned to realize substantial growth over the coming years, if not the next decade, that can deliver incremental improvements to margins. Given the strong market outlook, I am recommending PWR shares with a Strong Buy rating with a price target of $713/share at 26.76x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA.

Quanta Services Operational Update

The EPC market is the strongest it has ever been, with a vast amount of large-scale projects set to deliver growth over the next decade. While data center development has been front and center in recent years as a large growth market opportunity, industrial reshoring for pharmaceuticals, manufacturing, automotive production, and semiconductor fabrication are all driving elevated demand for construction and infrastructure services. The central theme is that the growth trajectory for reindustrialization is outpacing load capacity, resulting in the need for substantial investments for power transmission & distribution infrastructure development.

Thematically, I have reason to believe natural gas will be the cornerstone of growth for the coming years, given the growing demand for gas-fired power generation. Accordingly, GE Vernova (GEV) had 83GW gas turbine capacity in its backlog and slot reservations as of Q4 ’25, up from 62GW in Q3 ’25 and 55GW in Q2 ’25. While capacity largely ties to large-scale data center development, much of this is destined for grid support and industrial demand.

With an increasing outlook for gas power demand, Quanta should be in a strong position to service both the high-voltage electrical equipment for the utility and also the distribution and substation equipment at the data center site, as well as the gas pipeline to ensure stable load capacity. At these facilities, Quanta will focus most heavily on the mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) components of the projects. To deliver on this growth, Quanta has planned $500-700mm in supply chain investments to double its existing high-voltage transformer manufacturing capacity by 2028.

Bolstering its position in mechanical and process infrastructure, Quanta acquired Dynamic Systems in mid-2025, solidifying its exposure in the technology, semiconductor, healthcare, and other growth markets in the U.S. Accordingly, the acquisition enhanced Quanta’s supply chain for preconstruction fabrication and aftermarket services.

Quanta recently announced a major development, deepening its partnership with NiSource (NI) as part of Quanta’s total solutions power generation platform, providing an integrated approach to power generation and supporting infrastructure. In October 2025, Quanta was selected to demonstrate this platform with NiSource for the design, procurement, and construction of 3GW of load capacity, expanding its generation and grid infrastructure in power generation, battery energy storage, transmission, substations, and underground infrastructure to support a large-scale data center campus in Indiana. This project includes the delivery of combined-cycle gas turbines. The project is expected to commence in 2026 following regulatory approvals and is expected to be completed by 2032. The project will be undertaken as a 50/50 joint venture between Quanta and Zachry Group, proportionally splitting financial contributions through the JV. According to commentary provided at Quanta’s 2026 Investor Day, the partnership can deliver $5-7b over the course of 5-7 years.

Looking at the total market opportunity, management estimates the total addressable market from 2026-2030 will be $2.365T, up from $970b in the previous 4 years. A substantial proportion of the TAM resides in electrical distribution and transmission, two core markets Quanta operates in. This is, in part, underpinned by aging infrastructure that may need to be modernized to support higher power capacity required to facilitate electrification and load diversification.

At the macroeconomic level, high-voltage equipment and construction labor are presently in short supply, potentially pushing out projects over longer durations if equipment and personnel cannot be adequately sourced. This, in part, is a large value driver for Quanta’s investment in its high-voltage transformer manufacturing capacity, which may be well timed to meet new capacity needs in the market. Given the shortage in electrical equipment, Quanta could potentially see positive pricing power for infrastructure that can potentially drive margins over the coming quarters.

Quanta Services Financial Position & Forecast

Looking ahead to Q1 ’26, I’m forecasting Quanta to generate $7.3b in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $2.28/share. With the strong market opportunity at hand, I’m forecasting Quanta’s growth to remain durable for eFY26, particularly when considering its $44b backlog.

Looking at the balance sheet, Quanta remains well capitalized with $440mm in cash & equivalents and $6b in debt on the balance sheet for a net leverage ratio of 1.93x net debt/aEBITDA, within range of management’s 1.5-2x leverage target ratio. This should provide the firm with exceptional flexibility to continue its growth trajectory through organic reinvestments and targeted acquisitions to bolster its power transmission capabilities.

Risks Related to Quanta Services

Bull Case

Quanta has a rich backlog predicated on substantial demand for power infrastructure, supported by a multi-year data center build-out theme. Through both organic and inorganic growth, I believe Quanta will have the scale to take on high-growth, high-margin opportunities across the power value chain.

Bear Case

Uncertainty in the market remains heightened as to how much growth remains in the AI data center market. If growth in the market were to wane, I believe power infrastructure will follow suit, given that a large proportion of the new capacity expected to come online over the coming years is tied to large-scale data center development. A tight labor market may hinder growth in the near future.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

PWR shares are currently trading at 30.90x EV/aEBITDA, a relatively rich premium when compared to the shares’ historical trading range. Despite the elevated premium, I believe the market sees heightened growth for the EPC firm, underpinned by an expected $1.1T in power infrastructure investments by 2030 to support reindustrialization and data center development.

With a deep backlog and high demand for critical infrastructure, I believe Quanta is well positioned to further broaden its scope in these high-growth markets. I am recommending PWR shares with a Strong Buy rating with a price target of $713/share at 26.76x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: Laureate Education Teaches What Value Is
data.description:
On a personal level, I am not a fan of the for-profit education space. Having said that, I believe it is a fascinating industry to play in. The opportunities for attractive upside are definitely there. And with some companies, the growth rate is undeniably impressive. One good example of this can be seen by looking at Laureate Education (LAUR), a rather sizable player with a market capitalization as of this writing of $5.05 billion. Over the last few years, management has really increased the size of the business on both the top and bottom lines. Moving forward, I expect this trend to continue as well. When you add on top of this how the stock is priced currently, a "Buy" rating makes all the sense in the world.

Of course, this picture could change as new data comes in. And it just so happens that, on April 30, the management team at the company is expected to announce financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. We should expect continued expansion during this time. But if something unexpected comes out of the woodwork, my assessment could change.

An interesting education provider

For those who have never heard of Laureate Education before, it's helpful to know that the company focuses on providing certain education-related services from institutions that it has throughout Mexico and Peru. Management collectively refers to these as its Laureate International Universities network. At the end of the 2025 fiscal year, the company had five different institutions that boasted more than 50 campuses. Combined, they had about 497,700 students. And those students focus on a wide array of educational paths. Through these multi-year degree programs, they focus on fields such as medicine and health sciences, engineering and information technology, and business and management. These are the three largest disciplines that the company has, accounting for around 70% of its enrollments. Other meaningful areas would be architecture, art, and design, communications, education, and law and legal studies.

These are not just any run-of-the-mill universities. For the nations in which they operate in, they are considered high quality. For instance, the company’s Universidad Peruana de Ciencias Aplicadas institution was ranked number one in the educational sector in Peru in the most recent year for which data is available. It has 78,400 individuals enrolled in it. The largest of its institutions is Universidad Tecnologica de Mexico, which had 137,200 students enrolled. It is the largest private university in Mexico, though it only receives three out of five stars in terms of rating.

So quality does differ from location to location. The good news is that, of the other three institutions, even though one of them is not rated, the other two both get four stars, while the first one I mentioned gets five. While the majority of these students who attend these institutions are undergraduates, as is often the case with any university or higher educational institution, about 7% are graduate students. 8% are high schoolers, while 5% are technical or vocational students. So there is some diversity here.

Over the last few years now, management has been able to achieve some rather attractive growth. From 2023 through 2025, revenue jumped from $1.48 billion to $1.70 billion. As revenue grew, profitability followed suit. Net income nearly tripled from $107.6 million to $281.6 million. On an adjusted basis, it rose from $107.6 million to $255.9 million. Operating cash flow grew nicely from $250.8 million to $366.2 million. And if we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $294.6 million to $507.3 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $418.6 million to $518.9 million.

Drilling down into these performance figures is probably a wise idea. In the chart above, you can see the overall trajectory that the company has experienced in Mexico. Over the last three years, revenue jumped 12.1%, from $782.6 million to $877.4 million. And with that increase came a surge in profitability from $177 million to $229.4 million. That's an increase of 29.6% over that window of time.

According to management, most of this growth was organic in nature. Organic enrollment was responsible for $109.4 million of revenue growth over the last three years. A change in product mix, pricing, and the timing of revenue being recognized, added another $51.8 million to the top line. In fact, things would have been better had it not been for the fact that foreign currency fluctuations negatively impacted the company to the tune of $66.4 million when it comes to its operations in Mexico alone. When it comes to overall enrollment, the rise for the company in Mexico is from 242,000 to 269,400 over the last three years. This has come in large part from the addition of new programs that the company offers.

We have seen similar outcomes when it comes to its operations in Peru. From 2023 through 2025, revenue jumped 17.5% from $701.7 million to $824.4 million. Unfortunately, profitability growth was more modest here, with segment EBITDA growing only 14.5%, from $286.9 million to $328.6 million. Not surprisingly, organic enrollment growth was the main driver here, adding $56.6 million of the $122.7 million in revenue that the company experienced during this time. This came as the number of enrollees grew from 206,900 to 228,300. Changes in pricing, product mix, and timing ended up being responsible for another $23.5 million. While foreign currency fluctuations negatively affected the company in 2024, in 2025 they actually added $45.4 million to the top line.

Long term, I believe that the future for the company is bright. At least for the next couple of decades, demographics are working in the company's favor. From 1955 to 2025, Mexico saw its population explode from 31.65 million to 131.95 million. That's an annualized growth rate of 2.06%. And over the next couple of decades, through 2050, it should grow at another 0.49% per annum to hit 148.95 million. Peru saw its population grow at an annualized rate of 1.97% over the last 70 years. And over the next 25 years, it's expected to grow by 0.64% per annum. That would take it up to 40.58 million. By comparison, over the next 25 years, the population of the United States is expected to rise by only 0.37% per annum, taking the population from 347.28 million to 380.85 million. Generally speaking, population tends to slow as countries become wealthier. More developed nations need a more educated workforce, which should only increase demand for services the likes of which Laureate Education offers.

In the short run, management seems optimistic as well. For 2026, they expect revenue of between $1.89 billion and $1.91 billion. At the midpoint, this would mean a year-over-year growth rate of 11.5%. Total enrollment for the year should range between 516,000 and 521,000. At the midpoint, that would mean a year-over-year growth rate of 4.2%. As revenue increases, profitability should expand also. The expectation is for EBITDA to grow from $518.9 million in 2025 to between $583 million and $593 million this year. Adjusted earnings per share, meanwhile, should be between $1.95 and $2.03. Hitting the midpoint there would mean an adjusted net income of $295.9 million. And that would likely translate to adjusted operating cash flow of about $586.6 million.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart below how shares of the company are priced. You can also see how it's priced using results from 2025. In that same chart, I also stacked the business up against five other similar firms. What I found here was that it is priced slightly below the average. Two of the five companies that I compared it to are cheaper than it is on both a price-to-earnings basis and on an EV-to-EBITDA basis. And only one of them is cheaper when it comes to the price-to-operating cash flow approach. What's more, on an absolute basis, the stock falls in the undervalued category as I typically describe it.

As I mentioned at the start of this article, my opinion on the matter could change as new data comes in. And it just so happens that, before the market opens on April 30, management is expected to announce financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. In the table below, you can see what analysts expect compared to what the company achieved at the same time in 2025. You can also see what management has provided in terms of an estimate when it comes to revenue and EBITDA. On the whole, this looks to me like a business that actually could see a bit of weakness in terms of profitability. But revenue growth is still slated to occur. Although it is disappointing to see weaker expected profitability, it seems to stem, according to management, from a change in timing that will push around $9 million of revenue and the profitability associated with it from the first quarter of the year into the second quarter or beyond. Some of the impact will also come from investments in new campuses that the company has made as well. So I view this as more of a temporary bump in the road than something more concerning.

Takeaway

It is my assertion that Laureate Education makes for an interesting prospect. It is a high-quality player in the for-profit education market. Management has done an exceptional job of growing the company in recent years, and the long-term picture for the business looks bright. Given this combination of factors, on top of how the stock is priced, I have no choice but to rate it a "Buy" right now.

data.title: Nvidia And Marvell: A Match Made In Heaven
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) and Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL) announced a major strategic partnership on March 31, 2026, which will also result in Nvidia making a $2.0B equity investment in the AI infrastructure company as well. The deal is based on Nvidia opening up its NVLink ecosystem to Marvell, which will then be able to build semi-custom AI infrastructure for its Data Center clients and integrate it seamlessly with Nvidia's GPU, networking, and storage platforms. NVLink is key for Nvidia to overcome communication bottlenecks inherent in traditional PCIe connections and is often referred to as Nvidia's nervous system. The deal is a positive, especially for Marvell, given its much smaller size, but Nvidia is also set to benefit from greater adoption of Nvidia NVLink Fusion, a tool that allows partner companies to tailor their own AI products around Nvidia’s rapidly-evolving and dominant AI technologies.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Nvidia a Strong Buy consistently throughout 2025, including in my last coverage of the AI GPU maker in February—Growth Acceleration, FCF Surge, Agentic AI—right after the chip-maker reported blowout fourth-quarter earnings. Nvidia, as the leading global GPU company, is set to benefit from its partnership with Marvell through the growing adoption of its Nvidia NVLink platform, while Marvell is partnering with the biggest AI enterprise in the world, which will boost its XPU business. I previously also covered Marvell Technologies and rated the AI infrastructure company a strong buy, mainly because of the massive TAM expansion of the Data Center market: My Top AI Investment Pick For 2026.

Partnership and $2.0B strategic investment

Nvidia and Marvell announced a major strategic partnership on Tuesday that highlighted the growing need of AI companies to integrate their technologies in order to offer hyperscalers better inference performance. According to the press statement from Marvell, Nvidia will provide rack-scale AI compute, especially NVLink Interconnects and switches, while Marvell will focus on the supply of custom XPUs and NVLink Fusion-compatible scale-up networking.

Marvell's core business revolves around so-called XPUs—chips tailored for specific tasks like AI inference or data processing—that can now sit right next to an Nvidia H100 or Blackwell GPU, without experiencing data processing bottlenecks. For Marvell, the key benefit of this strategic partnership is that it allows the AI infrastructure company to integrate its custom chips and high-speed networking directly with Nvidia’s large AI-centric compute ecosystem. This integration could especially allow Marvell Technologies to capture a larger share of the AI factory market in the future and lead to an acceleration of its top-line growth.

NVLink is Nvidia’s high-speed, wire-based communications protocol designed specifically to scale up the performance of multi-GPU systems. NVLink is critical to the deployment of AI factories... which is why adoption of this platform is critical to Nvidia's future growth. By allowing Marvell to use NVLink, Nvidia is making it possible for different types of chips (including Marvell's custom processors and Nvidia's GPUs) to communicate instantly. This setup is set to improve data transfer and inference speeds and could make both Marvell's XPUs more compelling for hyperscalers.

Marvell is already experiencing a massive upsurge in its core Data Center business, mainly because the industry is ramping up its AI buildout plans. Since Marvell is providing critical, in-demand XPUs to run AI applications, Marvell has seen a very impressive ramp in revenues, gross profits, and net income in the last three years.

The deal with Nvidia is set to accelerate Marvell's growth trajectory following the partnership, as it can now more effectively penetrate the AI factory market, utilizing Nvidia's NVLink technology. Further, Nvidia is investing $2.0B in equity capital into Marvell... which is a good use of capital for both companies:

  1. Nvidia can deploy its excess cash resources into new, promising AI infrastructure companies while leveraging their custom silicon tech.
  2. Marvell just received a huge vote of confidence from the leading GPU design company in the world, allowing it to access its rapidly-evolving NVLink Fusion rack-scale platform.

Nvidia’s valuation

Nvidia shares surged more than 5% following the news, while Marvell's shares soared 13%, although Tuesday’s broad market rally helped all AI infrastructure companies, including Nebius (NBIS) and CoreWeave (CRWV), to reprice higher.

Shares of Nvidia have so far revalued 6.5% lower in 2026, which is in large part due to a shift in investor perceptions about artificial intelligence technologies. While the market was widely enthusiastic about the potential of AI in the 2023-2025 period, which obviously helped leading GPU makers like Nvidia, the market’s focus recently shifted towards the risks represented by AI.

These risks came to the forefront this year when Block (XYZ) announced a 40% headcount reduction as part of its AI-native strategy. Further, AI caused concerns in the private credit market, where a lot of investment firms have handed out big loans to SaaS companies whose very business models may see headwinds from artificial intelligence advancements.

Nvidia is currently valued at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 15.7X, which I feel is inadequate for a company that dominates 90% of the GPU market and is expected to grow its revenues more than 70% in the current fiscal year. Nvidia, given a higher historical P/E ratio of 19.4X, is exceptionally cheap right now (19% discount to the 3-year average P/E ratio), making the value proposition not only compelling from a deal point of view. Shares of Marvell are priced at 18.2X forward earnings, which reflects a 32% discount from the 3-year average P/E ratio. Both Nvidia and Marvell are now severely mispriced, given their respective historical contexts.

Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), a major competitor for Nvidia, due to the company’s aggressive foray into the Data Center GPU market and growing success in it through releases such as the MI350 AI accelerator, is trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 18.9X.

Marvell itself has suffered a valuation correction to the downside in 2026 because of a more critical attitude of investors towards AI infrastructure companies, some of which are driving aggressive CapEx programs financed with debt. The strategic alignment between Nvidia and Marvell, I believe, actually ends up limiting risks for investors here, in my opinion, and I would not be surprised to see follow-up agreements between the two companies and a much larger equity investment from Nvidia.

Risks with Nvidia

The risk for Nvidia, in the long term, may be that its own GPU sales are going to get cannibalized as a result of this deal. Marvell's custom XPUs are mainly sold to hyperscalers, and if Nvidia allows these XPUs to be used in its server racks, companies like Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG), or Microsoft (MSFT) may have less incentive to buy more expensive GPUs from Nvidia in the future. For Marvell, the main risk is that it attaches itself to Nvidia's NVLink platform strategy, which may make its custom XPUs less attractive for customers who want to remain vendor-neutral going forward.

Final thoughts

Nvidia just executed another very solid partnership deal with up-and-coming AI infrastructure company Marvell, whose main focus is the manufacturing of custom-built XPUs. Via the NVLink connection, Nvidia can offer its customers access to Marvell’s custom silicon technology while improving inference and data processing speeds. The partnership between the two companies will also lead to a $2.0B investment from Nvidia, which is a good use of Nvidia's excess cash.

The most obvious benefit for Marvell is its ability to tap into Nvidia’s nervous system, its NVLink Fusion platform, which could allow the AI infrastructure company to accelerate its growth in the AI factory market. The deal between Nvidia and Marvell is a match made in heaven, in my opinion, and should help both companies cement their leadership in the AI industry.

data.title: John Hancock U.S. Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • U.S. stocks rose in Q4, supported by strong corporate earnings and consumer spending, as well as solid economic growth.
  • The fund outperformed the Russell 1000 Growth Index, helped the most by favorable security selection in the information technology, industrials, and communication services sectors. Picks in consumer discretionary detracted.
  • In positioning the portfolio for continued economic acceleration, the fund was overweight its growth and valuation upside factors and underweight its total capital return and quality factors.

Market review and outlook

U.S. equities rose in Q4, bolstered by robust corporate earnings, resilient consumer spending, and solid economic growth, even as anxiety about lofty valuations among tech stocks and concern about massive spending on artificial intelligence supported value and cyclical sectors over their growth counterparts. The cooling labor market and worries about inflation risks also kept the markets on edge, even as faster-than-expected economic growth supported equities. The U.S. Federal Reserve reduced its benchmark short-term interest rate by a quarter point at each of its two meetings in Q4. However, policymakers were divided on their outlook for interest rates amid considerable uncertainty about inflation and employment.

As of year-end, we maintain a strong outlook, as uncertainty about central bank policy has eased and global monetary and fiscal measures remain supportive, though recent labor data has softened. Given improving investor sentiment and ongoing divergence among equity markets, we maintain a constructive outlook that, in our view, may provide a favorable environment for bottom-up stock selection.

Contributors and detractors

In Q4, the fund outperformed the benchmark, driven by favorable security selection, especially in the information technology, industrials, and communication services sectors, partly offset by picks in consumer discretionary. In addition, sector allocation, which derives from our bottom-up (stock-by-stock) investment approach, modestly contributed.

Compared with the index, the fund's largest individual relative contributor was not holding weak-performing benchmark component Oracle Corp (ORCL). The stock declined amid concern about the company's AI business, ongoing legal challenges, and a high level of debt. Other notable contributors in Q4 included overweights in Alphabet, Inc. (GOOG), parent company of Google, and drug maker Eli Lilly & Company (LLY).

In contrast, the fund's top individual relative detractor was Pool Corp (POOL), a distributor of swimming pool supplies. Its stock fell during the quarter even as the company reported better-than-anticipated earnings, perhaps because the company's selling and administrative expenses increased and operating margin remained flat. Other notable detractors of the fourth quarter were chip maker Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. and gaming company DraftKings, Inc. (DKNG), the latter of which was no longer held in the portfolio at quarter end.

Portfolio changes

With recent improvements across key economic indicators, we believe it is prudent to position the portfolio for continued economic acceleration. Accordingly, we remain overweight the fund's growth and valuation upside factors (30% of the portfolio each) and underweight the fund's total capital return and quality factors (20% each). Within this positioning, we continue to lean into what we believe are high-quality, durable growth names.

As of year-end, the fund's largest sector overweights were in financials and energy. The fund's largest underweights were in industrials and communication services.
